title: Jeffrey Dahmer — The Making of a Monster
description: Between 1978 and 1991, Jeffrey Dahmer murdered 17 men and boys in Wisconsin, committing acts of cannibalism and necrophilia. He was caught by accident when a victim escaped and flagged down police. Dahmer confessed to everything. His case raised profound questions about how a monster is made — and how society's failures enabled him.

segments.voice: Milwaukee, Wisconsin. July 22, 1991. Two police officers respond to a call about a man running in the street with handcuffs dangling from his wrist. The man is Tracy Edwards, 32 years old, visibly shaken. He tells officers that a man named Jeff had lured him back to his apartment, drugged him, and held him at knifepoint for four hours. He leads police to a yellow apartment complex on North 25th Street. What they find inside will haunt them for the rest of their lives.
segments.visual: Milwaukee Wisconsin apartment complex 1991 police patrol

segments.voice: In Jeffrey Dahmer's one-bedroom apartment, police found photographs of dismembered bodies. A human head in the refrigerator. Three more heads in the freezer. A barrel containing acid and dissolving body parts. A box of human skulls. A human heart wrapped in paper. The remains of eleven men. The officers who responded to that call were not prepared for what they found. No one could have been.
segments.visual: forensic investigation apartment evidence dark disturbing

segments.voice: Jeffrey Lionel Dahmer was born on May 21, 1960, in Milwaukee. His childhood has been extensively documented — a distant father, a mentally ill mother, a series of moves, social isolation, and an early fascination with the bones and internal structures of roadkill he would find and dissect. Psychologists who have studied his case describe a child who was curious about the inside of things, who connected vulnerability with intimacy in deeply disordered ways.
segments.visual: suburban childhood Ohio Wisconsin isolation youth

segments.voice: His parents' marriage deteriorated. His mother Lionel later gave extensive interviews about the family's dysfunction. His first murder occurred when he was 18 — Steven Hicks, a young hitchhiker he brought home when his parents were away. He killed Hicks, buried the body, later dug it up and dissolved the remains with acid. Then he stopped, for nine years.
segments.visual: young man 18 years old isolated rural Ohio 1978

segments.voice: Dahmer joined the Army, was discharged for alcohol abuse, lived with his grandmother in Wisconsin, and continued to drift. He was convicted of sexually assaulting a 13-year-old boy in 1988 and received a conviction and a work release sentence — meaning he served his time while continuing to live in the community. He murdered his second victim the same year he was convicted of the assault.
segments.visual: army discharge alcohol abuse grandmother Wisconsin

segments.voice: Between 1987 and 1991, Dahmer murdered 16 more men and boys — nearly all of them Black, gay, or both. He targeted men at gay bars, shopping malls, and bus stops. He lured them with money, alcohol, or the promise of modeling work for his photography. He drugged them with sleeping pills dissolved in their drinks. Then he strangled, dismembered, and in some cases consumed portions of their remains.
segments.visual: gay bar Milwaukee 1980s lure targeting prey

segments.voice: Dahmer kept skulls as trophies. He told investigators that he believed by eating parts of his victims, he was ensuring they stayed with him forever — that they became part of him and could not leave. The psychology of possession and control that drove his crimes was, by any clinical measure, among the most extreme documented in the history of criminal psychology.

In May 1991, a deeply disturbing event occurred. A 14-year-old boy named Konerak Sinthasomphone was found drugged and disoriented on the street outside Dahmer's apartment building. Two women called 911. Police arrived. Dahmer told them Konerak was his 19-year-old boyfriend who had had too much to drink. Despite the women's protests — despite Konerak's visible distress — police returned the boy to Dahmer's apartment. Konerak was murdered that night.
segments.visual: police failure returned victim 911 call neighbor women

segments.voice: The Konerak Sinthasomphone incident became one of the most scrutinized police failures in modern American criminal history. The two officers, John Balcerzak and Joseph Gabrish, were fired after Dahmer's arrest but later rehired after arbitration. Their failure — which investigators attributed to homophobia and racial bias — allowed one more murder to occur. It was not the only moment when the system failed.
segments.visual: police accountability race bias failure system

segments.voice: When Tracy Edwards escaped and police entered the apartment, Dahmer did not flee or resist. He was calm. He cooperated with his arrest. He then gave one of the most extensive and detailed confessions in the history of American criminal justice — describing every murder, every victim, every act, methodically and without apparent emotion. He seemed, by multiple accounts, almost relieved.
segments.visual: confession interview calm cooperation detailed account

segments.voice: Dahmer's trial in January 1992 rested on a single question: was he legally insane? His attorneys argued that only a profoundly insane man could have committed these acts — that he suffered from paraphilic disorders so severe that he could not conform his behavior to the law. The prosecution argued that his careful planning, his attempts to cover his crimes, and his functional everyday life demonstrated sanity.
segments.visual: insanity defense trial legal psychiatry debate

segments.voice: The jury deliberated for five hours and found Dahmer sane on all 15 charges for which he stood trial. He was convicted and sentenced to 15 consecutive life terms — 957 years in prison. At his sentencing, he expressed genuine remorse. He said he wished he could give his life to bring back his victims' lives. Whether that remorse was genuine or performed is something only he knew.
segments.visual: verdict sentencing 15 life terms 957 years prison

segments.voice: In prison, Dahmer eventually agreed to be baptized by a Christian minister. He was reportedly quiet, studious, and cooperative. He read the Bible. He made religious artwork out of ketchup and mustard packets. On November 28, 1994, while cleaning a gymnasium with two other inmates, he was beaten to death by Christopher Scarver, who used a metal bar. He was 34 years old.

Christopher Scarver later gave interviews explaining why he killed Dahmer. He said Dahmer had taunted other prisoners with crude food sculptures resembling dismembered body parts. Scarver, who was serving time for murder, decided he had had enough. Whether this account is accurate or self-serving has been debated.
segments.visual: prison inmate testimony motive killing discussion

segments.voice: The families of Dahmer's victims have never received the acknowledgment they deserve. The majority of his victims were Black men — a demographic largely absent from the intense media coverage his case received. When Milwaukee police returned Konerak Sinthasomphone to Dahmer, neighbors who had tried to intervene were dismissed. The question of whether race and sexuality influenced whose safety was taken seriously remains deeply uncomfortable.
segments.visual: victims families Black men Milwaukee memorial recognition

segments.voice: The Netflix series DAHMER released in 2022 brought his story to a new global audience. Families of victims were deeply critical — arguing they had not been consulted, that the series sensationalized and re-traumatized them. The show's popularity reignited a debate about where responsible documentation ends and exploitation begins in the true crime genre.
segments.visual: Netflix controversy victim family criticism documentary ethics

segments.voice: Jeffrey Dahmer is one of the most studied figures in criminal psychology. His case has generated hundreds of academic papers, books, and courses on the intersection of childhood trauma, psychological disorder, and extreme violence. It has also served as a case study in how systemic failures — inadequate parole supervision, biased policing, overwhelmed social services — can enable a killer to operate long after warning signs have appeared.
segments.visual: criminal psychology academic research trauma study

segments.voice: Seventeen men and boys are dead. Their names were Steven Hicks, Steven Tuomi, James Doxtator, Richard Guerrero, Anthony Sears, Ricky Beeks, Edward Smith, Ernest Miller, David Thomas, Curtis Straughter, Errol Lindsey, Tony Hughes, Konerak Sinthasomphone, Matt Turner, Jeremiah Weinberger, Oliver Lacy, and Joseph Bradehoft. They were human beings. Not props for a mythology of evil. People who deserved to come home.
segments.visual: seventeen names listed memorial tribute victims remembered
